original_abstract:
Years after its implementation, the application of the so-called Decommunization Act has revealed a critical legal tension, recently highlighted by several rulings from the Supreme Administrative Court of Poland. Central to this debate is the scope of a voivode’s duty to independently investigate the facts in decommunization proceedings, particularly in relation to the expert opinions provided by the Institute of National Remembrance (IPN). The investigative responsibilities of the voivodes largely hinge on the legal weight assigned to these opinions; if they are not considered legally binding, then voivodes must independently evaluate the historical and factual circumstances of each case, even when the IPN has already issued a formal stance.

Current judicial trends suggest that the Supreme Administrative Court views IPN opinions as merely one component of the evidentiary record, subject to independent verification by the voivode. While some theoretical frameworks suggest this verification should be limited to methodological consistency, practical application has increasingly allowed for substantive challenges to the IPN’s findings. 

This study critiques this prevailing judicial approach. The author contends that an analysis of the Decommunization Act’s legislative intent reveals that the IPN’s opinions were intended to be binding upon voivodes. Given the IPN’s specialized expertise and statutory mandate—as opposed to the general administrative competencies of the voivodes—these opinions should not be treated as ordinary evidence requiring outside validation. Instead, from a systemic perspective, they should be regarded as authoritative declarations of fact from a competent state body, acting as a specialized partner to the voivodes in the decommunization process.
humanized_abstract:
Years after it was introduced, controversy over the implementation of the so-called Decommunization Act has come to the surface, as recent decisions from the Supreme Administrative Court in Poland have pointed out a major conflict that has arisen in the context of changing interpretations of the Act. In particular, the issue at the heart of this controversy is whether or how far the voivodes who are engaged in the process of decommunization have their own burden of proof to prove the facts, taking into account the role of expert opinions issued by the Institute of National Remembrance (IPN). The scope of the obligations of the voivodes depend on whether the opinions of IPN have a binding character. If not, then voivodes will have to assess the historical and factual character of individual cases on their own, regardless of the opinions expressed by IPN.

Recent case law from the Supreme Administrative Court (SAC) suggests that its doctrine on views provided by the Institute of National Remembrance (IPN) has shifted towards the IPN’s opinions not being treated as conclusive proof of the facts set out in such views, but merely as part of the body of evidence. The voivode has to verify them. As a matter of doctrine theory of verification of opinions provided by IPN should not exceed the issue of methodological appropriateness; however, in practice, the SAC and other administrative courts more and more often apply the material criteria (grounds) to verify IPN’s views.

This paper criticizes the current judicial doctrine. It argues that the legislative intent of the Act on Restoring the Integrity of the State, and consequently the opinions of the Institute of National Remembrance (IPN) adopted for this purpose, were to constitute binding directives for the voivodes. Given IPN’s specific expertise, as well as its position as the body entrusted with the care for memory of the nation, as opposed to the generally administrative competences of the voivodes, the opinions may not be treated as simply another type of evidence, the authenticity of which should be proved by other individuals or entities. From the systemic perspective, the IPN opinions should be considered as simple official statements of fact, issued in their capacity as an organ of state specialising in matters of state memory and collaborating with the voivodes in the realisation of the task of decommunisation.
